Context: Create domain-specific exception classes for different error types in your application
Do: Define exception classes per domain area with error codes and context data
Dont: Use generic Exception or RuntimeException for everything
Code Bad: \n        public readonly int $requested
column11: throw new \\Exception('Order not found');\n// or\nthrow new \\RuntimeException('Not enough stock');

Context: Configure a central exception handler to transform exceptions into appropriate HTTP responses
Do: Map domain exceptions to HTTP status codes in the exception handler. Log internally. Return JSON for API
Dont: Let exceptions bubble up unhandled showing stack traces to users
Code Bad: // No custom handler, default Laravel shows:\n// Whoops! Something went wrong.\n// or stack traces in debug mode exposed to users

Context: Catch the most specific exception first and handle each type appropriately
Do: Catch specific exception types in order from most to least specific. Always handle or rethrow
Dont: Catch generic Exception and ignore it or catch everything in one block

Context: Return structured validation errors with field-level messages and a 422 status code
Do: Use Laravel Form Requests which automatically return 422 with per-field errors
Dont: Return 400 with generic messages or a single error string

Context: Log errors once at the boundary with full context. Never log and rethrow at every level
Do: Log errors at the handler/controller level with request context, user ID, and correlation ID
Dont: Log the same error at every layer creating duplicate log entries

Context: Catch specific database exceptions and convert them to meaningful domain errors
Do: Catch QueryException and check error codes for constraint violations unique violations etc
Dont: Let PDO exceptions bubble up to the user with SQL details

Context: Return all API errors in a consistent JSON format with error code message and optional details
Do: Define a standard error response structure and use it everywhere
Dont: Return different shapes from different endpoints (sometimes string, sometimes object)

Context: Create a base exception for your domain and extend from it for specific errors
Do: Define a base DomainException and derive specific exceptions. Use interfaces as markers for HTTP status mapping
Dont: Have all exceptions inherit directly from PHP base Exception or RuntimeException

Context: Validate input at the boundary and fail immediately with clear error messages
Do: Validate as early as possible (Form Requests route model binding). Reject bad input before processing
Dont: Process data halfway then discover it is invalid and try to roll back

Context: Configure PHP error reporting differently for development and production environments
Do: In production: log all errors but display none. In development: display all. Use APP_DEBUG=false in production
Dont: Leave display_errors=On in production exposing internal info

Context: Wrap database operations in transactions and handle failures with proper rollback
Do: Use DB::transaction() which auto-rolls back on exception. Clean up side effects manually if needed
Dont: Make multiple DB changes without transactions leaving data inconsistent on failure

Context: Automatically retry operations that may fail due to transient issues like network timeouts
Do: Use retry() helper or custom retry logic with exponential backoff for external service calls
Dont: Fail immediately on first transient error or retry forever without limits

Context: When external services fail provide a degraded but functional experience rather than a hard error
Do: Implement fallbacks for non-critical services. Cache last known good values. Skip optional features on failure
Dont: Crash the entire request because one non-critical service is down

Context: Use Laravel abort helpers for quick HTTP exception responses in controllers
Do: Use abort() abort_if() abort_unless() for simple HTTP error responses in controllers
Dont: Write full response construction for simple error cases

Context: Add contextual information to exceptions as they propagate up the call stack
Do: Catch rethrow with additional context using previous exception chaining or custom properties
Dont: Return bare exceptions with no context making debugging difficult
Code Bad: \n                previous: $e

Context: Create self-reporting and self-rendering exceptions that know how to log and display themselves
Do: Implement report() and render() methods on custom exceptions for self-contained error handling
Dont: Handle all exception rendering in a single giant Handler class

Context: Create user-friendly error pages for common HTTP status codes in production
Do: Design custom error views for 404 403 419 429 500 503 that match your app design
Dont: Show default Laravel error pages or raw exception messages in production
Code Bad: // No custom error pages\n// Users see: 'Whoops, looks like something went wrong.'\n// Or worse: full stack trace with file paths and SQL queries

Context: Return generic auth error messages to prevent user enumeration and credential guessing
Do: Return identical error messages for wrong email AND wrong password. Log specific reason internally
Dont: Return different messages revealing whether email exists or which field is wrong

Context: Wrap third-party service exceptions into your domain exceptions to decouple layers
Do: Catch vendor-specific exceptions at the adapter level and throw your own domain exceptions
Dont: Let GuzzleException or StripeException leak through your service layer

Context: Handle errors in queued jobs with retry limits backoff and failed job logging
Do: Set $tries, $backoff, and implement failed() method on jobs for proper error handling
Dont: Let jobs fail silently or retry forever without limits
